feed_card.title: The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory?
feed_card.key_finding_one_line: The paper argues that perception, action, attention, and learning all reduce to minimizing one quantity, the variational free energy that upper-bounds the surprise of an agent's sensory states.
teasers: Can one equation explain the whole brain?
Why staying alive means resisting surprise
Perception and action, the same move

card_visual.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 56 32' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none' stroke='var(--accent, #73c28d)' stroke-width='3.5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'><circle cx='16' cy='16' r='6'/><circle cx='42' cy='16' r='6'/><path d='M22 16 H36'/></svg>

content.title: The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory?

authors.name: Karl J. Friston
authors.affiliation: Wellcome Trust Centre for Neuroimaging, Institute of Neurology, University College London

sections.content: A system that resists slipping into disorder keeps its sensory states within a narrow range, the same as keeping their surprise low. Surprise itself is out of reach: measuring it needs the intractable model evidence $p(\tilde{s} \mid m)$. So the agent minimizes variational free energy $F$ instead, an upper bound on surprise built only from quantities it already encodes. Lowering $F$ by revising beliefs is approximate Bayesian inference about the causes of sensation. Lowering it by acting on the world is behaviour. Perception and action turn out to be two routes to one quantity.

content.image_caption: The agent's internal states $\mu$ meet the world's hidden causes $\vartheta$ only across two channels: sensory states $s$ and active states $a$. Perception updates $\mu$ to predict the sensory input, and action changes the input to match the prediction. Each lowers $F$.

sections.content: By 2010 neuroscience had a drawer full of partial theories, each strong in its own corner. Predictive coding explained cortical hierarchies. The Bayesian brain explained perceptual inference. Reinforcement learning and optimal control handled action, and efficient coding handled sensory representation. Each one explained a single faculty and stopped at the border of the next. Friston asks a bolder question: is there one quantity the brain minimizes that gives rise to all of them at once? Stated precisely, is there a single objective function whose optimization yields Bayesian perception, adaptive action, attention, and learning, each as a special case of the same computation?

Strip away the neuroscience and the claim is about any system that lasts. To stay alive, you occupy a small set of states. Your body holds its temperature, pH, and glucose within narrow ranges and almost never drifts into states that would kill you. Call a state surprising if your body rarely visits it. Lasting, then, means keeping surprise low over time. The catch: a creature cannot measure its own surprise directly, since that would mean tallying every way the world might have produced what it senses. So it does something cheaper. It keeps an inner model of the world, tunes it, and acts to bring its surroundings into line with what the model predicts. Do both well and the creature behaves as if it were reasoning about hidden causes and working to survive, without ever computing surprise itself.

content.body: The pieces were already in place. Helmholtz argued in the 1860s that perception is unconscious inference, the brain's best guess at the hidden causes of its sensations. A century later this became the Bayesian brain: perception as the inversion of a generative model, computing a posterior over causes given sensory data. Rao and Ballard gave it a cortical implementation in 1999 with predictive coding, in which higher levels send predictions down and lower levels return only the prediction error. Separately, optimal control and reinforcement learning described action as the maximization of expected reward or the minimization of cost. These accounts shared a Bayesian and variational vocabulary but no common objective. Friston's earlier work on variational filtering and dynamic expectation maximization supplied the mathematical machinery, the free-energy bound on model evidence, that the 2010 paper uses to join them.

key_priors.claim: Perception is unconscious inference about the hidden causes of sensation.
The cortex implements perception as predictive coding, passing prediction errors up a hierarchy and predictions down.
The brain represents and computes with probability distributions, the Bayesian-brain hypothesis.
The Helmholtz machine learns a generative model by minimizing a free-energy bound, linking inference to variational methods.

content.body: The agent carries a generative model $m$, a joint density $p(\tilde{s}, \vartheta \mid m)$ over sensory states $\tilde{s}$ and their hidden causes $\vartheta$. Inference would require the posterior $p(\vartheta \mid \tilde{s}, m)$, which is intractable because the model evidence $p(\tilde{s} \mid m)$ in its denominator integrates over all possible causes. Variational Bayes sidesteps this by introducing a recognition density $q(\vartheta \mid \mu)$, a tractable approximation to the posterior parameterized by the agent's internal states $\mu$, which in the brain are physical quantities such as neuronal activity and synaptic strengths. Free energy $F$ is a functional of $q$ and the sensory states, and the agent has two ways to reduce it. It can change its internal states $\mu$, which improves the approximation $q$ to the true posterior, and this is perception. Or it can change action $a$, which alters the sensory states the agent samples, and this is behaviour. Friston casts both as gradient descent on the same $F$, run in generalized coordinates of motion, which lets the scheme track the full trajectory of states through time. The agent never has to evaluate surprise or the posterior. It only descends a gradient on a quantity built from its own model and its current sensations.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 96 400 92' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='10' y='120' width='104' height='60' rx='12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='148' y='120' width='104' height='60' rx='12' stroke='var(--accent, #73c28d)' stroke-width='2.5'/><rect x='286' y='120' width='104' height='60' rx='12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.8'/><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.55'><path d='M114 150 h34'/><path d='M148 150 l-11 -6 m11 6 l-11 6'/><path d='M252 150 h34'/><path d='M286 150 l-11 -6 m11 6 l-11 6'/></g><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' text-anchor='middle'><text x='62' y='145' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600'>Generative</text><text x='62' y='163' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' opacity='0.7' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace'>p(s,ϑ|m)</text><text x='200' y='145' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600'>Free energy</text><text x='200' y='163' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace'>F</text><text x='338' y='145' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' font-weight='600'>Recognition</text><text x='338' y='163' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' opacity='0.7' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace'>q ≈ posterior</text><text x='131' y='112' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' opacity='0.7'>define</text><text x='269' y='112' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' opacity='0.7'>descend ∂F</text></g></svg>

content.body: Free energy is the expected difference, under the recognition density, between the log of that density and the log of the generative model. Writing $\langle \cdot \rangle_{q}$ for expectation under $q$, three rearrangements of the same expression reveal what minimizing it accomplishes.

equations.label: Free energy
equations.description: Free energy is energy minus entropy. The first term is the energy, the expected negative log probability of sensations and their causes under the generative model, low when the model assigns high probability to what is sensed. The second term is the negative entropy of the recognition density. Every quantity on the right belongs to the agent's own model, which is why $F$ is computable where surprise is not.

equations.latex: F = D_{\mathrm{KL}}\big[\, q(\vartheta \mid \mu) \,\|\, p(\vartheta \mid \tilde{s}, m) \,\big] - \ln p(\tilde{s} \mid m)
equations.label: Divergence and evidence
equations.description: Rearranged this way, $F$ splits into the Kullback-Leibler divergence between the recognition density and the true posterior, plus the surprise $-\ln p(\tilde{s} \mid m)$. The divergence measures how far the agent's approximate belief sits from the posterior it is trying to recover. Driving $F$ down by changing internal states shrinks this divergence, so $q(\vartheta \mid \mu)$ converges on the true posterior. This is the formal sense in which perception is Bayesian inference.

equations.label: Bound on surprise
equations.description: A Kullback-Leibler divergence is never negative, so free energy sits above surprise for any recognition density, with equality exactly when $q$ matches the posterior. An agent that keeps $F$ low over time keeps surprise low over time without ever evaluating it, which is the property a system needs to stay within its viable states.

equations.latex: F = \underbrace{D_{\mathrm{KL}}\big[\, q(\vartheta \mid \mu) \,\|\, p(\vartheta \mid m) \,\big]}_{\text{complexity}} - \underbrace{\langle \ln p(\tilde{s} \mid \vartheta, m) \rangle_{q}}_{\text{accuracy}}
equations.label: Complexity minus accuracy
equations.description: A third rearrangement separates $F$ into complexity and accuracy. Accuracy is how well the inferred causes explain the sensations. Complexity is how far the recognition density has to move from the prior to achieve that fit. Minimizing free energy maximizes accuracy while paying a complexity cost for departing from prior belief, which is a precise statement of Occam's razor: the agent prefers the simplest explanation that fits.

equations.label: Perception and action
equations.description: The agent descends the free-energy gradient along two axes. Changing internal states $\mu$ optimizes the recognition density and is perception. Changing action $a$ changes the data the agent samples and is behaviour, because action enters $F$ only through the sensory states it produces, so $\partial_{a} F$ runs through $\partial \tilde{s} / \partial a$. Perception adjusts the model to fit the world. Action adjusts the world to fit the model. The two share one objective.

notation_legend.meaning: variational free energy, the quantity the agent minimizes
sensory states in generalized coordinates of motion (position, velocity, acceleration, and higher orders)
hidden causes in the environment that produce sensations
internal states of the agent, such as neuronal activity and synaptic strengths, that parameterize the recognition density
action states through which the agent changes its sensory input
the agent's generative model of how causes produce sensations
recognition density, the agent's approximate posterior over causes
the true posterior over causes, generally intractable
model evidence, whose negative logarithm is surprise
Kullback-Leibler divergence, a non-negative measure of how far one distribution sits from another

content.title: Free energy bounds surprise from above
content.finding: The governing inequality is $F \geq -\ln p(\tilde{s} \mid m)$, the gap being the divergence between the recognition density and the true posterior. Since that divergence is never negative, the bound holds for any recognition density and tightens to equality when $q$ matches the posterior. This piece is a theorem about probability, true independently of any claim about brains, and it is the load-bearing one: a computable $F$ can stand in for the surprise it sits above.

content.title: Minimizing free energy performs Bayesian inference
content.finding: Reduce $F$ by changing internal states alone and the divergence $D_{\mathrm{KL}}[\,q(\vartheta \mid \mu) \,\|\, p(\vartheta \mid \tilde{s})\,]$ falls, so the recognition density converges on the true posterior. Mathematically this is approximate Bayesian inference. Calling that convergence perception is the framework's reading of the same equations. Under linear, Gaussian assumptions the scheme reduces to predictive coding, with prediction errors weighted by their precision.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 24 400 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='110' y='36' width='180' height='52' rx='12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='110' y='160' width='180' height='52' rx='12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.8'/><g stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'><path d='M158 160 V92' stroke='var(--accent, #73c28d)' stroke-width='2.5'/><path d='M158 92 l-7 12 m7 -12 l7 12' stroke='var(--accent, #73c28d)' stroke-width='2.5'/><path d='M242 88 V156' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/><path d='M242 156 l-7 -12 m7 12 l7 -12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7'/></g><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' text-anchor='middle'><text x='200' y='67' fill='currentColor' font-size='14' font-weight='600'>higher level</text><text x='200' y='191' fill='currentColor' font-size='14' font-weight='600'>lower level</text><text x='76' y='114' fill='var(--accent, #73c28d)' font-size='13' font-weight='600'>prediction</text><text x='76' y='130' fill='var(--accent, #73c28d)' font-size='13' font-weight='600'>error</text><text x='76' y='146' fill='var(--accent, #73c28d)' font-size='13'>× precision</text><text x='322' y='126' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' opacity='0.7'>prediction</text></g></svg>

content.title: Action and perception minimize the same quantity
content.finding: The agent can also reduce $F$ by acting, since action changes the sensory states it samples. Behaviour becomes a form of inference: the agent moves so its sensations come to match its predictions. Friston calls this active inference, and on this account reflexes, motor control, and goal-directed action all derive from the single action term $\dot{a} = -\,\partial_{a} F$.

content.title: Attention falls out as precision weighting
content.finding: Free-energy minimization weights each prediction error by its expected precision, the inverse variance of the noise on that channel. Optimizing those precision parameters sets the gain on prediction-error units. On this account attention is that gain control inside the inference: judge a channel reliable and the weight on its errors rises.

content.title: One objective subsumes learning and value
content.finding: Over slower timescales the same descent on $F$ tunes the parameters of the generative model, which is learning, and the priors that encode preferred states, which takes over the job done by value and reward elsewhere. Perception, action, attention, learning, and value then read as five timescales of one optimization. This is the principle's broadest claim and its most disputed: the same generality that makes it a unification is what lets critics call it unfalsifiable. The complexity-accuracy split names the cost paid throughout: fit the data well while moving as little as possible from prior belief.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 24 400 196' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='44' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>minimize F</text><g stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'><path d='M120 172 V100' stroke='var(--accent, #73c28d)' stroke-width='2.5'/><path d='M120 100 l-8 13 m8 -13 l8 13' stroke='var(--accent, #73c28d)' stroke-width='2.5'/><path d='M280 100 V172' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5' opacity='0.85'/><path d='M280 172 l-8 -13 m8 13 l8 -13' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2.5' opacity='0.85'/></g><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' text-anchor='middle'><text x='120' y='192' fill='var(--accent, #73c28d)' font-size='15' font-weight='700'>accuracy</text><text x='120' y='210' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' opacity='0.7'>fit to data</text><text x='280' y='192' fill='currentColor' font-size='14' font-weight='600'>complexity</text><text x='280' y='210' fill='currentColor' font-size='13' opacity='0.7'>stray from prior</text></g></svg>

sections.content: A unifying account has to reproduce the theories it claims to contain. This one does. Under linear dynamics and Gaussian noise, free-energy minimization reduces to predictive coding, and in the static case to a Kalman filter, schemes whose neural and behavioural predictions are already tested. Relax the Gaussian assumption and more general Bayesian filters reappear. Run the same gradient descent on parameters instead of states and it yields the delta rule and Hebbian plasticity. Recovering each known scheme in its own limit is the precondition a unification has to meet. On its own it leaves the principle's broader claims still to be judged.

Friston is explicit that the principle is not a mechanistic theory and makes no predictions on its own. The free-energy bound is close to a mathematical tautology: any system at a non-equilibrium steady state can be described as if it minimizes free energy, so the formalism by itself does not say which systems are brains or how a particular brain works. The content lies entirely in the generative model, and the principle is silent on where that model comes from or what form it takes. He notes too that the theory fixes the quantity to be optimized but says nothing about the priors that define an agent's preferred states, which have to be supplied separately, for instance by evolution. He presents it as a constraint any plausible brain theory must satisfy, leaving the model of cognition itself to be filled in.

Critics outside the programme have pressed harder. Colombo and Series argued in 2012 that the Bayesian-brain commitments the principle leans on are underdetermined by the neural evidence. A recurring objection, raised in different forms by Colombo and others, is that the principle risks unfalsifiability: if every persisting system can be redescribed as minimizing free energy, the claim explains everything and forbids nothing. The dark-room problem is the sharpest version. If an agent only minimizes surprise, why does it not retreat to a dark, silent room where sensations are perfectly predictable? Proponents answer that prior expectations encode the states an agent expects to occupy, so a creature that expects to forage finds a dark room highly surprising. Critics reply that this rescues the theory only by loading the answer into priors the principle does not itself constrain. Whether the free-energy principle is a substantive empirical theory or a powerful modelling language remains open.

For neuroscience the principle reorganizes a scattered field around one computation. Predictive coding stops being one model among many and becomes the form free-energy minimization takes in a cortical hierarchy, which predicts specific microcircuitry: superficial pyramidal cells carrying prediction errors upward, deep cells carrying predictions downward, and neuromodulators such as dopamine and acetylcholine setting the precision the formalism requires. Attention becomes a precision parameter, which ties attentional and perceptual deficits to a common failure of precision estimation, a framing now used to model hallucinations and the symptoms of schizophrenia and autism as aberrant precision. Action and perception share a currency, so motor control and perceptual inference can be written with the same equations. The principle turns separate research programmes into special cases of one message-passing scheme, and it shifts the thing you must specify to explain a particular brain function from the mechanism to the generative model.

The free-energy bound is the same object machine learning minimizes as the evidence lower bound, so a variational autoencoder trained with the ELBO and a brain minimizing free energy optimize identical quantities, with the recognition density playing the encoder and the generative model the decoder. This identity made active inference a route to building agents that explore and act under one objective, an alternative to reward-only reinforcement learning. In physics the principle connects to the statistics of systems held away from thermodynamic equilibrium, where a Markov blanket separating internal from external states is the condition for a system to possess anything describable as beliefs, a line Friston later developed toward a physics of self-organization. In biology the same Markov-blanket argument applies below the brain, to single cells and to morphogenesis, suggesting inference-like dynamics are a general feature of things that hold a boundary against their environment. The exchange has run both ways: variational Bayes gave neuroscience its objective, and the neuroscience gave machine learning a reason to take active, model-based agents seriously.

content.question: Why can a biological agent not minimize surprise directly?
options: Surprise is a subjective feeling that cannot be quantified
Evaluating surprise requires the model evidence, an integral over all possible causes that is intractable
Surprise depends on future sensations the agent has not yet received
The brain lacks the precision to represent negative log probabilities
content.explanation: Surprise is $-\ln p(\tilde{s} \mid m)$, and the evidence $p(\tilde{s} \mid m)$ marginalizes over every hidden cause, which is the integral variational methods exist to avoid. Free energy substitutes a computable bound for it.

content.question: Free energy equals the divergence between the recognition density and the true posterior plus surprise. What follows from that divergence being non-negative?
options: Free energy is a lower bound on surprise
Surprise can be negative whenever the divergence is large
Free energy is an upper bound on surprise, tight when the recognition density matches the posterior
Free energy equals surprise only when the prior equals the likelihood
content.explanation: A non-negative divergence sits on top of surprise, so $F \geq -\ln p(\tilde{s} \mid m)$. The bound is tight exactly when $q(\vartheta \mid \mu)$ equals $p(\vartheta \mid \tilde{s}, m)$ and the divergence vanishes.

content.question: In the complexity-accuracy decomposition, what does the complexity term measure?
options: The number of parameters in the generative model
The computational time required to invert the model
The entropy of the sensory states
The divergence of the recognition density from the prior, the cost of moving belief to fit the data
content.explanation: Complexity is $D_{\mathrm{KL}}[\,q(\vartheta \mid \mu) \,\|\, p(\vartheta \mid m)\,]$, how far posterior belief departs from the prior. Minimizing free energy trades accuracy against this cost, which is why the scheme enacts Occam's razor.

content.question: On this account, what distinguishes perception from action?
options: Perception changes internal states to fit the model to sensations; action changes sensations to fit the model, both minimizing $F$
Perception minimizes free energy, while action maximizes a separate reward function
Perception is Bayesian, while action is governed by classical optimal control
Perception runs on fast timescales and action only on slow, evolutionary ones
content.explanation: Both descend the same free-energy gradient. Updating $\mu$ improves the fit of the recognition density to the data, and changing $a$ resamples the data toward the predictions. The dual descent $\dot{\mu} = -\,\partial_{\mu} F$, $\dot{a} = -\,\partial_{a} F$ is the whole of active inference.

content.question: The principle identifies attention with precision. What does precision weighting do in the scheme?
options: It selects which generative model the agent uses
It determines the prior probability of each hidden cause
It fixes the learning rate for synaptic plasticity alone
It sets the gain on prediction-error signals according to their estimated reliability
content.explanation: Precision is the inverse variance of the noise, and free-energy minimization weights each prediction error by it. Estimating precision raises or lowers the gain on error units, the operation the theory equates with attention.

content.question: What does the dark-room problem claim, and how do proponents answer it?
options: That the brain cannot represent darkness; proponents reply that vision is not required
That free energy diverges when sensory input is zero; proponents add a regularization term
That a surprise-minimizing agent should seek a perfectly predictable dark room; proponents reply that prior expectations make such a room highly surprising
That the model evidence is undefined in the dark; proponents add a constant
content.explanation: If agents only minimized surprise, a constant dark room would be optimal. The reply is that an agent's priors encode the states it expects to occupy, so a foraging creature finds the dark room surprising. Critics note this works only by loading the result into priors the principle does not constrain.

content.question: Which statement matches Friston's own framing of the principle's main limitation?
options: The bound is close to a tautology, so the explanatory content sits in the generative model, which the principle does not itself specify
The principle predicts behaviour precisely but cannot be tested with current imaging
The principle covers perception but breaks down for action
Free-energy minimization holds only for linear, Gaussian systems
content.explanation: Friston presents the principle as a constraint any brain theory must satisfy, not a mechanistic model. Because almost any persisting system can be described as minimizing free energy, the substance lies in the particular generative model and priors, which sit outside the principle.

content.name: Karl J. Friston
content.one_line: Neuroscientist who introduced statistical parametric mapping, the standard analysis method in functional neuroimaging, and went on to propose the free-energy principle.
content.affiliation: Wellcome Trust Centre for Neuroimaging, University College London

sections.content: The lineage runs from Helmholtz through twentieth-century cybernetics to Friston's own methods work. Friston had already reshaped neuroimaging once, with statistical parametric mapping, the standard analysis across functional brain imaging. Through the 2000s he developed variational schemes for inverting dynamic models of brain responses, dynamic expectation maximization and generalized filtering, which produced the free-energy bound as a by-product of fitting models to data. The 2010 review gathered a decade of that work and a longer prehistory into one claim: that the bound the methods minimized was not only a tool for analysing brains but a principle the brains themselves obey. The paper sits at the head of what became active inference, computational psychiatry, and, more speculatively, a proposed physics of self-organizing systems.

content.label: Friston K. The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory? Nature Reviews Neuroscience 2010;11(2):127-138
Friston K, Kilner J, Harrison L. A free energy principle for the brain. Journal of Physiology-Paris 2006;100(1-3):70-87
Rao RPN, Ballard DH. Predictive coding in the visual cortex. Nature Neuroscience 1999;2(1):79-87
Bogacz R. A tutorial on the free-energy framework for modelling perception and learning. Journal of Mathematical Psychology 2017;76:198-211
Colombo M, Series P. Bayes in the brain: on Bayesian modelling in neuroscience. British Journal for the Philosophy of Science 2012;63(3):697-723
